region_of_origin: Asia (broadly); commercially produced and popular in East and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for flavoured packaged vinegar drinks; closest match is the general vinegar drink concept under FOODON_03301099.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers vinegar-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for flavoured packaged RTD vinegar drinks. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured vinegar drink as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged bottle of flavoured vinegar drink is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing about 105 kcal and 22.7 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured vinegar drink (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured vinegar drink (packaged), cr_id D05010017. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened fruit vinegar drink
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged flavoured vinegar drinks in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary) based on analogous sugar-sweetened fruit juice/vinegar beverages with similar sugar content (~9 g/100 g) and acetic acid content. Acetic acid in vinegar is documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA), which may lower effective GI below that of equivalent sugar-only beverages. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (26.1 g) / 100 = 13.6. Serving size used: 250 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.06 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 20 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this drink due to its high sugar content (9.06 g/100 g), which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite the low sodium level of 20 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.06 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; rapid glucose absorption despite vinegar's mild glucose-attenuating effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged flavoured vinegar drink because its 9.06 g sugar/100 g with zero dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the acetic acid content does not sufficiently offset the high sugar load in this commercial formulation, and plain diluted apple cider vinegar without added sugar is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (9.06 g/100 g) in liquid form promotes triglyceride elevation and metabolic risk; fat and sodium are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this drink because regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration (9.06 g/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiometabolic risk, even though fat content is zero and sodium is low at 20 mg/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content (9.06 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this drink as its high refined sugar content (9.06 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation and exacerbates joint symptoms, with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients such as omega-3, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre present in this formulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Vinegar Drink (Packaged) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink beverage made from diluted vinegar (typically fruit or grain-based acetic acid) combined with added flavours and sugar, sold in sealed bottles or cartons. It is popular across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, South Korea, Japan, and Taiwan, where vinegar-based health drinks have a long cultural tradition. The drink has a mildly tart, sweet-sour flavour profile with a thin, watery texture. Per 100 g, it provides 42 kcal, 10.44 g carbohydrate (of which 9.06 g is sugar), 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 20 mg sodium. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a sugar-and-acid-based aqueous beverage with no protein or lipid ingredients. Dietary fibre is zero by formulation. The high sugar content (9.06 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which carries clinical concern for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Despite vinegar's documented mild glucose-attenuating properties via acetic acid, the high sugar load in this packaged commercial form negates that benefit. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-medium boundary) based on analogous sugar-sweetened fruit vinegar beverages, with a glycemic load of approximately 13.6 per 250 ml serving. This drink is not recommended for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, or cardiovascular disease. Plain diluted apple cider vinegar without added sugar is a clinically preferable alternative for those seeking vinegar's metabolic benefits.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010017) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact entry. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: Zero-macro justification (g4): protein_g is naturally zero as this is an aqueous vinegar-and-sugar beverage containing no protein ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — no lipid ingredients are present. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a clear liquid beverage with no fibre-containing ingredients. Negative sentinel values (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this specific packaged flavoured vinegar drink are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this product category as of 2026. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 per the HPB source. GI note: No direct GI measurement exists for this product; GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on similar sugar-sweetened vinegar beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Johnston et al., 2004). Ontology note: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for flavoured packaged RTD vinegar drinks. Multilingual note: This is a generic packaged commercial beverage with no single cuisine origin; no mandatory local-language names apply beyond English. The product is sold across multiple Asian markets without a single canonical local-language name.
